quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electric potential (voltage)"?

options.text: V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)
Light nuclei combine: ²H + ³H → ⁴He + n + 17.6 MeV; requires extreme temperatures (>10⁷ K) to overcome Coulomb barrier
Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
ΔxΔp ≥ ℏ/2; fundamental limit on simultaneous measurement precision; not due to instrument limitations but inherent to nature

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electric potential (voltage)" is defined as: V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Potential difference"?

options.text: ΔV = -∫E·ds; the work per unit charge needed to move a charge between two points; what batteries provide
Total A (mass number), total Z (charge), total energy (including mc²), and total momentum are all conserved
P = εσAT⁴; total power radiated by a body; P ∝ T⁴ means doubling temperature increases radiation 16-fold
Point fingers in direction of v (or I), curl toward B; thumb points in force direction for positive charge

quiz_questions.explanation: "Potential difference" is defined as: ΔV = -∫E·ds; the work per unit charge needed to move a charge between two points; what batteries provide. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Equipotential surfaces"?

options.text: Objects underwater appear shallower than they are due to refraction: apparent depth = actual depth × (n₂/n₁)
Node at closed end, antinode at open: f_n = nv/(4L), n = 1,3,5,... (odd harmonics only); e.g., clarinet, stopped pipe
Produced by hot dense matter (solids, liquids, dense gases); all wavelengths present; intensity varies with temperature (blackbody)
Surfaces of constant V; perpendicular to field lines; no work done moving charge along them

quiz_questions.explanation: "Equipotential surfaces" is defined as: Surfaces of constant V; perpendicular to field lines; no work done moving charge along them. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "E-V relationship"?

options.text: Angular momentum L = nℏ (n = 1,2,3,...); equivalently, nλ = 2πr (standing wave condition for electron orbit)
Converging mirror: f > 0; produces real images when object is beyond F; virtual images when object is inside F
Zero electrical resistance below a critical temperature T_c; enables powerful electromagnets (MRI: 1.5-7T fields) and lossless power transmission
E = -dV/dx (or E = -∇V in 3D); E points from high V to low V; stronger field where equipotentials are closer together

quiz_questions.explanation: "E-V relationship" is defined as: E = -dV/dx (or E = -∇V in 3D); E points from high V to low V; stronger field where equipotentials are closer together. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Electric potential energy"?

options.text: U = kq₁q₂/r for two point charges; positive when charges have same sign (repulsive); negative when opposite (attractive)
Series: R_total = R₁ + R₂; same current through each. Parallel: 1/R_total = 1/R₁ + 1/R₂; same voltage across each
v = fλ; wave speed equals frequency times wavelength; speed depends on the medium, not the wave itself
Strong attraction and permanent magnetization; magnetic domains align cooperatively; responsible for permanent magnets

quiz_questions.explanation: "Electric potential energy" is defined as: U = kq₁q₂/r for two point charges; positive when charges have same sign (repulsive); negative when opposite (attractive). The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "V = U/q = kQ/r for a point charge; scalar quantity measured in volts (V = J/C)"?

options.text: Thin-film interference
De Broglie wavelength
Dispersion
Electric potential (voltage)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electric potential (voltage)".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "ΔV = -∫E·ds; the work per unit charge needed to move a charge between two points; what batteries provide"?

options.text: Doppler effect
Second Law of Thermodynamics
Ohm's law
Potential difference

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Potential difference".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Surfaces of constant V; perpendicular to field lines; no work done moving charge along them"?

options.text: Compound microscope
Snell's law
Curie temperature
Equipotential surfaces

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Equipotential surfaces".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "E = -dV/dx (or E = -∇V in 3D); E points from high V to low V; stronger field where equipotentials are closer together"?

options.text: Electric current
Thin-film interference
Dispersion
E-V relationship

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "E-V relationship".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "U = kq₁q₂/r for two point charges; positive when charges have same sign (repulsive); negative when opposite (attractive)"?

options.text: Mass spectrometer
Compound microscope
Electric field
Electric potential energy

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Electric potential energy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Electric Potential is accurate?

options.text: Electromagnetic interactions are one of the four fundamental forces of nature.
Electric Potential relates to the potential energy per unit charge in an electric field.
Beta-plus decay (β⁺): proton → neutron + positron + neutrino (Z→Z-1).
The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electric Potential as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Electric Potential?

options.text: The electromagnetic force governs the behavior of all charged matter, from electrons in atoms to current in wires.
Voltage (electric potential difference) is ΔV = ΔU/q = -∫E·ds.
Curie temperature: above this temperature, ferromagnets lose their permanent magnetism and become paramagnetic.
Radioactivity and Decay describes the spontaneous transformation of unstable nuclei.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electric Potential as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Electric Potential?

options.text: Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.
Power dissipated: P = IV = I²R = V²/R.
For a point charge: V = kQ/r.
Beta-plus decay (β⁺): proton → neutron + positron + neutrino (Z→Z-1).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electric Potential as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Electric Potential?

options.text: Equipotential surfaces are perpendicular to electric field lines and represent locations of equal potential.
Superposition: the net electric field from multiple charges is the vector sum of individual fields.
An emission spectrum consists of bright lines at specific wavelengths produced when excited gas atoms emit photons during electron transitions downward.
In hydrogen: E_n = -13.6 eV/n².

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electric Potential as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Electric Potential?

options.text: When E and B are perpendicular, a velocity selector filters particles: only those with v = E/B pass through undeflected (electric and magnetic forces balance).
Moving a charge along an equipotential requires no work.
Electrons and Atomic Models traces the evolution of atomic theory from Thomson's plum pudding model to Bohr's quantized orbits to the quantum mechanical model.
Magnetic Fields and Forces explores the interaction between magnetic fields and charged particles.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Electric Potential as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Electric Potential?

options.text: Prescription eyeglasses: optometrists measure required correction in diopters; a -3.0 D prescription means a diverging lens with f = -0.33 m for myopia correction
Batteries maintain a fixed potential difference (EMF) between terminals; the chemical reaction supplies energy to move charges from low to high potential
Camera lens design: modern camera lenses contain 10-20 elements to correct for aberrations, provide zoom capability, and maintain image sharpness across the frame
Night vision devices use the photoelectric effect: photons from dim scenes eject electrons from a photocathode, which are then amplified to produce a visible image

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electric Potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Electric Potential?

options.text: Household wiring: outlets are wired in parallel (each at 120V) with circuit breakers in series (trip at 15-20A) to prevent overheating and fires
Defibrillators deliver a large potential difference (~1000-3000 V) across the heart to reset its electrical rhythm during cardiac arrest
Fluorescent lighting: UV photons from mercury vapor excite phosphor coating electrons, which emit visible light as they return to lower energy levels
LED color selection: the semiconductor band gap determines the emitted photon wavelength; engineers tune the gap to produce specific colors (red, blue, green, white)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electric Potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Electric Potential applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: LED circuits: resistors are placed in series with LEDs to limit current (V_supply - V_LED)/R = I, preventing the LED from burning out
Van de Graaff generators accumulate charge on a conducting sphere, creating potentials exceeding 100,000 V—used in nuclear physics and dramatic classroom demonstrations
Seismology: P-waves (longitudinal) and S-waves (transverse) travel through Earth at different speeds, allowing scientists to locate earthquake epicenters and map Earth's interior structure
PET-CT combines metabolic imaging (PET using ¹⁸F-FDG) with anatomical imaging (CT X-rays) for precise cancer diagnosis and treatment monitoring

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electric Potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Electric Potential?

options.text: Neon signs: specific gases glow with characteristic colors because their unique energy level spacings produce photons at specific visible wavelengths
Camera lens design: modern camera lenses contain 10-20 elements to correct for aberrations, provide zoom capability, and maintain image sharpness across the frame
Batteries maintain a fixed potential difference (EMF) between terminals; the chemical reaction supplies energy to move charges from low to high potential
Noise barriers on highways: effective at blocking high-frequency (short λ) sound but less effective for low-frequency (long λ) sound that diffracts over the top

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electric Potential.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Electric Potential in practice?

options.text: Wireless charging (Qi standard): a changing magnetic field in the charging pad induces an EMF in the receiving coil inside the phone
Noise-canceling headphones use destructive interference: a microphone detects ambient sound, and speakers produce the inverted wave to cancel noise
The James Webb Space Telescope uses a 6.5m segmented gold-coated mirror to collect infrared light from the earliest galaxies in the universe
Defibrillators deliver a large potential difference (~1000-3000 V) across the heart to reset its electrical rhythm during cardiac arrest

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Electric Potential.
